name: Eswatini
tagline: The kingdom of the Lion and the She-Elephant, Africa's last absolute monarchy
summary: Eswatini, known as Swaziland until 2018, grew from the southward movement of Nguni-speaking peoples into south-eastern Africa and the rise of the Dlamini royal house. Tradition regards Ngwane III, in the mid-eighteenth century, as the first king of the Swazi, whose followers took the name bakaNgwane. During the upheavals of the mfecane, Sobhuza I consolidated a kingdom in the centre of the country, and his son Mswati II expanded it to its greatest extent in the mid-nineteenth century; from him the Swazi take their name. The kingdom is governed through a dual monarchy of the king, the Ngwenyama ('the Lion'), and the queen mother, the Ndlovukati ('the She-Elephant'). In the 1880s King Mbandzeni granted a flood of land, grazing and mineral concessions to Boer and British fortune-seekers, opening an era of foreign control. Administered first through the South African Republic and, after the Second Boer War, directly by Britain as a High Commission Territory, Swaziland was kept out of the Union of South Africa and gained independence from the United Kingdom on 6 September 1968 under King Sobhuza II. In 1973 Sobhuza repealed the independence constitution and ruled by decree; his successor Mswati III, crowned in 1986, presides over one of the world's last absolute monarchies, shaken by pro-democracy protests in 2021.

founding.label: Independence from the United Kingdom
founding.detail: The British protectorate of Swaziland became an independent kingdom on 6 September 1968, with King Sobhuza II as head of state under a Westminster-style constitution. Britain had administered the territory as a High Commission Territory and, despite provisions for its possible transfer, never incorporated it into South Africa.

quickFacts.label: Government
quickFacts.value: Absolute monarchy; dual monarchy of Ngwenyama and Ndlovukati

eras.title: Nguni Migrations and the House of Dlamini
eras.standfirst: Nguni-speaking peoples move south into the region, and the Dlamini royal house gives rise to the first king of the Swazi.
body: The deep human history of the region reaches back to early farming and ironworking communities, but the modern Swazi nation traces its origins to the southward movement of Bantu-speaking, Nguni peoples into south-eastern Africa over several centuries. The Dlamini royal lineage, which still rules today, remembers a migration that brought the clan southward toward the coast of present-day Mozambique and then inland. By the early eighteenth century the Dlamini, under leaders such as Dlamini III, had settled near the Pongola River where it cuts through the Lubombo Mountains.

From this base emerged the figure later tradition names as the first king of the modern Swazi: Ngwane III, who is generally placed in the mid-eighteenth century. He led his people across and along the Pongola and established a kingdom whose followers became known as the bakaNgwane, the country itself being called kaNgwane after him. He is remembered as having set his capital at Shiselweni, in the south of present-day Eswatini, the cradle from which the kingdom would expand northward.

These foundational dates rest largely on oral tradition and royal genealogy rather than written record, and historians treat the precise years with caution. What is clear is that by the later eighteenth century a recognisable Dlamini-led polity existed in the south of the country, organised around cattle, chieftaincy and the royal house, and poised to be drawn into the turbulent currents that would soon reshape all of southern Africa.

events.title: Nguni peoples move south
events.summary: Bantu-speaking Nguni groups, among them the ancestors of the Dlamini, migrate southward into south-eastern Africa over the following centuries.

events.title: Reign of Ngwane III
events.summary: Ngwane III, traditionally regarded as the first king of the modern Swazi, leads his followers across the Pongola and is remembered for founding the kingdom, with a capital at Shiselweni.

pullquote.text: For his name the people were called bakaNgwane and the country kaNgwane: Ngwane III is remembered as the founder of the modern Swazi nation.
pullquote.attribution: On the origins of the kingdom

eras.title: The Mfecane and the Making of a Nation
eras.standfirst: Amid the upheavals of the mfecane, Sobhuza I consolidates a kingdom and his son Mswati II expands it to its height, giving the Swazi their name.
body: In the early nineteenth century the rise of the Zulu kingdom set off the mfecane ('the crushing'), a chain of wars, migrations and famine that convulsed southern Africa. Sobhuza I, also called Somhlolo, became king on the death of his father Ndvungunye around 1815. Pressed by the powerful Ndwandwe under Zwide, he moved his people and capital northward into central Eswatini, where after the collapse of the Ndwandwe he established a settled kingdom centred on the Ezulwini valley. Through war, diplomacy and marriage alliance he drew Nguni, Sotho and remaining San communities into a single political order, founding the modern Swazi state.

His son Mswati II, who came to the throne around 1840 and ruled effectively from the mid-1840s until his death in 1868, was remembered as the greatest of the Swazi fighting kings. He reorganised the army on the Zulu model of age-regiments rather than clan levies, and used it to expand the kingdom to its greatest extent, from the Pongola in the south toward the Sabie in the north, doubling its territory through campaigns against neighbouring peoples and extending Swazi influence as far as Delagoa Bay. It is from Mswati II that the Swazi take their name.

It was in this era that the distinctive structure of Swazi kingship took its mature form: a dual monarchy in which the king, the Ngwenyama ('the Lion'), ruled jointly with the queen mother, the Ndlovukati ('the She-Elephant'). The king was the administrative and military head of the nation, while the Ndlovukati held spiritual authority, controlling ritual functions central to rainmaking and to the incwala ceremony of national renewal, and acting as regent when there was no reigning king. The two offices were conceived as a balance of power at the heart of the state.

events.title: Accession of Sobhuza I
events.summary: On the death of Ndvungunye, Somhlolo becomes king as Sobhuza I and soon faces the pressures of the mfecane and the Ndwandwe under Zwide.

events.title: Migration into central Eswatini
events.summary: Attacked by the Ndwandwe, Sobhuza I moves his people north and establishes a kingdom centred on the Ezulwini valley, the heartland of the Swazi nation.

events.title: Reign of Mswati II begins
events.summary: Mswati II succeeds Sobhuza I; he reorganises the army into age-regiments and expands the kingdom to its greatest extent. The Swazi take their name from him.

events.title: Death of Mswati II
events.summary: The death of Mswati II ends the era of Swazi conquest and expansion, leaving a unified nation at its widest territorial reach.

figures.name: Sobhuza I (Somhlolo)
figures.blurb: Consolidated the modern Swazi kingdom during the mfecane, moving north under Ndwandwe pressure to settle the Ezulwini valley and welding diverse peoples into one nation.

figures.name: Mswati II
figures.blurb: Greatest of the Swazi fighting kings, who reorganised the army and expanded the kingdom to its height; the Swazi nation is named after him.

pullquote.text: The king rules as the Ngwenyama, the Lion; the queen mother as the Ndlovukati, the She-Elephant. The two offices were conceived as a balance at the heart of the nation.

eras.title: The Concessions Era and Foreign Control
eras.standfirst: King Mbandzeni grants away land and mineral rights to European fortune-seekers, opening the way to administration by the South African Republic and then Britain.
body: Under King Mbandzeni, who reigned from 1875 to 1889, a flood of Europeans descended on the kingdom seeking land, grazing, mining and trading rights. Advised by the agent Theophilus 'Offy' Shepstone, Mbandzeni granted concession after concession; by the late 1880s he drew a substantial annual income from rents and dues, but the documents, many signed with a cross by a king who could neither read nor write, granted far more than he understood. The concessions overlapped and covered nearly the whole country, mortgaging Swazi land and resources to outsiders.

The independence of the kingdom had been formally guaranteed by Britain and the Transvaal in conventions of 1881 and 1884, but the chaos of the concessions persuaded both powers that some control was needed. A provisional government representing Swazi, British and Transvaal interests was set up in 1890, and from 1894 the South African Republic (the Transvaal) undertook the administration of Swaziland as a protectorate, while the kingdom retained nominal autonomy. Swazi rulers protested and appealed against the loss of their land, but the structures of foreign control hardened.

The South African (Second Boer) War of 1899 to 1902 swept this arrangement away. After the British victory the administration of Swaziland passed to the British governor of the Transvaal, and in 1906 it was transferred to the High Commissioner for South Africa, making Swaziland a High Commission Territory alongside Bechuanaland and Basutoland. The concessions left a lasting wound: a large share of Swazi land was now held by Europeans, a grievance that would shape the kingdom's politics for generations.

events.title: Mbandzeni becomes king
events.summary: Mbandzeni (Dlamini IV) ascends the throne; his reign coincides with an influx of European concession-seekers into the kingdom.

events.title: The concessions era
events.summary: Mbandzeni grants extensive land, grazing and mineral concessions to Boer and British fortune-seekers, ceding control over most of the kingdom's land and resources.

events.title: Protectorate of the South African Republic
events.summary: By convention the Transvaal (South African Republic) takes over the administration of Swaziland as a protectorate, while the kingdom keeps nominal autonomy.

events.title: A High Commission Territory
events.summary: After the Second Boer War, control of Swaziland passes from the Transvaal governor to the High Commissioner for South Africa, making it a High Commission Territory under direct British oversight.

figures.name: Mbandzeni (Dlamini IV)
figures.blurb: Son of Mswati II whose reign saw the granting of sweeping land and mineral concessions to Europeans, opening the era of foreign control over the kingdom.

pullquote.text: The concessions, many signed with a cross by a king who could not read, mortgaged almost the whole of Swaziland to outsiders.
pullquote.attribution: On the concessions era of the 1880s

eras.title: British Territory and the Road to Independence
eras.standfirst: Kept out of the Union of South Africa, the protectorate endures decades of British rule before Sobhuza II leads it to independence.
body: As a High Commission Territory, Swaziland was governed by Britain but deliberately kept separate from the Union of South Africa, established in 1910. The South Africa Act of 1909 had made provision for the eventual transfer of Swaziland, Bechuanaland and Basutoland to the Union, and South Africa repeatedly pressed for it; but the imperial government declined, and as apartheid took hold after 1948 transfer became politically impossible. The territory thus remained under direct British administration, with a resident commissioner reporting to the High Commissioner.

Through these decades the Swazi monarchy worked steadily to recover what the concessions had lost. King Sobhuza II, who became Ngwenyama in 1921 and would reign for some six decades, made the restoration of Swazi land a central cause, sending a deputation to London and pursuing the buy-back of European-held farms so that, over time, much of the country returned to Swazi hands. The dual monarchy and traditional institutions endured beneath the colonial structure.

Constitutional advance came in the 1960s. A constitution was introduced in 1963 and revised in 1967, and elections were held in which the king's Imbokodvo National Movement, drawing on traditional loyalties, swept the polls. On 6 September 1968 Swaziland became fully independent from the United Kingdom, a kingdom under a Westminster-style constitution with Sobhuza II as head of state. Unusually among Britain's African colonies, it entered independence with its precolonial monarchy intact and at the centre of national life.

events.title: Kept out of the Union of South Africa
events.summary: When the Union of South Africa is formed, Swaziland remains under direct British control; despite later South African requests, the territory is never transferred.

events.title: Sobhuza II becomes Ngwenyama
events.summary: Sobhuza II assumes the kingship and makes the recovery of Swazi land lost in the concessions a defining cause of his long reign.

events.title: Self-government and elections
events.summary: Under a revised constitution Swaziland gains internal self-government; the king's Imbokodvo National Movement wins the elections, setting the stage for independence.

events.title: Independence of Swaziland
events.summary: Swaziland becomes an independent kingdom, free of British rule, with Sobhuza II as head of state under a Westminster-style constitution.

pullquote.text: Unusually among Britain's African territories, Swaziland entered independence with its precolonial monarchy intact and at the centre of the new state.

eras.title: Rule by Decree and the Birth of Eswatini
eras.standfirst: Sobhuza II repeals the constitution and rules by decree; Mswati III inherits an absolute monarchy that renames itself Eswatini and faces calls for democracy.
body: The independence constitution lasted barely five years. On 12 April 1973 King Sobhuza II repealed it, dissolved parliament, banned political parties and assumed supreme power, declaring that the Westminster framework was alien to Swazi tradition. He thereafter ruled by decree, and a new constitution drawn up in the later 1970s entrenched the king as absolute ruler, governing alongside traditional structures such as the tinkhundla system of local councils. Sobhuza II reigned until his death in 1982, one of the longest-reigning monarchs in recorded history.

After a regency, his son was crowned as King Mswati III on 25 April 1986 at the age of eighteen, with his mother Ntfombi serving as Ndlovukati. Mswati III has continued to rule as an absolute monarch: he appoints the prime minister and government, political parties remain effectively barred from contesting power, and the elected portion of parliament holds little real authority. In 2005 a new constitution was adopted, but it preserved the king's dominant position.

In April 2018, marking both his fiftieth birthday and the fiftieth anniversary of independence, Mswati III renamed the country from the Kingdom of Swaziland to the Kingdom of Eswatini, the siSwati name meaning 'land of the Swazis', and ending recurrent confusion with Switzerland. Eswatini remains widely described as Africa's, and one of the world's, last absolute monarchies. That status was challenged in mid-2021, when pro-democracy protests, sparked in part by the death of a law student, spread across the country and met a deadly security crackdown, leaving demands for reform unresolved.

events.title: Sobhuza II repeals the constitution
events.summary: King Sobhuza II repeals the independence constitution, dissolves parliament and bans political parties, ruling thereafter by decree as an absolute monarch.

events.title: Death of Sobhuza II
events.summary: Sobhuza II dies after a reign of some sixty years, among the longest in recorded history, leaving the throne to a regency.

events.title: Coronation of Mswati III
events.summary: Aged eighteen, Mswati III is crowned king, continuing the absolute monarchy with his mother Ntfombi as Ndlovukati.

events.title: Swaziland becomes Eswatini
events.summary: On the fiftieth anniversary of independence, Mswati III renames the country the Kingdom of Eswatini, reviving its siSwati name.

events.title: Pro-democracy protests
events.summary: Pro-democracy protests sweep Eswatini and are met by a deadly crackdown, the most serious challenge yet to one of the world's last absolute monarchies.

figures.name: King Sobhuza II
figures.blurb: Reigned for some six decades, campaigned to recover Swazi land, led the country to independence in 1968, and in 1973 repealed the constitution to rule by decree.

figures.name: King Mswati III
figures.blurb: Crowned in 1986, he rules as an absolute monarch; in 2018 he renamed Swaziland as Eswatini, and faced major pro-democracy protests in 2021.

pullquote.text: In 2018, on the fiftieth anniversary of independence, the king renamed Swaziland as Eswatini, 'land of the Swazis'.

figures.name: Mswati II
figures.blurb: The greatest of the Swazi fighting kings, who expanded the kingdom to its widest extent in the mid-nineteenth century; the Swazi nation takes its name from him.

figures.name: King Sobhuza II
figures.role: Ngwenyama; founding head of independent Swaziland
figures.blurb: One of history's longest-reigning monarchs; he recovered much Swazi land, led the country to independence in 1968, and repealed the constitution in 1973 to rule absolutely.

figures.name: King Mswati III
figures.blurb: Crowned in 1986, he renamed Swaziland as Eswatini in 2018 and rules one of the world's last absolute monarchies, confronted by pro-democracy protests in 2021.
